sources: Avoma note: 'Primary driver to change: Ariba charges based on spend in platform; intent to move PR/PO flow into SAP to avoid those fees' (26 Jun)
metrics.narrative: QuantumScape processes approximately 9,000 purchase orders per year with a 50/50 split between SG&A overhead and long-tail materials/equipment/R&D purchasing. Benjamin stated on 26 Jun that the primary pain is SAP Ariba's spend-based charging model: 'SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP.' The goal is to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees by moving PR creation into SAP ERP while retaining an intake/orchestration layer. Procurement is currently decentralized—engineers and scientists execute full purchasing (requisitions, supplier onboarding, follow-up)—causing control gaps and heavy audit overhead for the small procurement team. Benjamin stated: 'as a result of that, we as a procurement organization have been needing to audit them and make sure that they are making the requisition right...we're wanting to let them handle a big portion of the purchasing process, but we need some tools on our side to set them up for success.' The quantified impact is the Ariba spend-based fees (dollar amount not disclosed) plus procurement audit labor inefficiency. The compelling event is the upcoming Ariba renewal (date unknown), which will force a re-evaluation of intake providers.

sources: Avoma notes: 'Buyer responded positively to Teams-native intake and intuitive UI; acknowledged Zycus features compare favorably to Zip' (26 Jun)
champion.narrative: Champion is developing but weak. Benjamin Sheres (Procurement Operations Manager, technical contact) is engaged and validated Merlin Intake fit positively on the 26 Jun discovery call ('that was all interesting', 'nice to keep you in mind for the future', acknowledged Teams-native intake and intuitive UI compare favorably to Zip). He is the pain owner and process champion, driving procurement automation and centralization efforts at QuantumScape. However, Benjamin has no budget authority, no access to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker, and explicitly stated he cannot advance the deal now: 'I don't think that is in the books right now...I don't think that we're gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now...just like with Zip, it's still nice to keep you in mind for the future.' His engagement is passive and exploratory—he is building awareness of options but cannot drive a decision without EB/DM alignment. Benjamin has not introduced Zycus to CFO, VP Finance, IT leadership, or Supply Chain leadership, and no evidence of internal advocacy exists. The champion is at risk of going passive: the deal is on hold awaiting the Ariba renewal (date unknown), and Benjamin asked Zycus to 'stay in touch' via LinkedIn, webinars, and product updates rather than committing to a next meeting or stakeholder introduction. To strengthen the champion, Zycus must help Benjamin build the business case (quantify Ariba cost savings, ROI, procurement efficiency gains) and coach him to introduce Zycus to the Economic Buyer when the renewal triggers evaluation.

sources: Avoma notes: 'Current incumbent: SAP Ariba (Guide Buying)—significant existing investment; charging model is a pain point' and 'Alternative evaluated: Zip—buyer viewed it positively but switching costs/internal factors prevented adoption' (26 Jun)
competition.narrative: Competition is confirmed but not a live multi-vendor fight—this is an incumbent displacement scenario. SAP Ariba (Guide Buying) is the incumbent and the primary barrier. Benjamin stated on 26 Jun: 'we've already made significant investment in getting Ariba working' and prior evaluation of Zip was blocked by 'internal organizational factors' (high switching cost due to Ariba investment). Ariba's pain point is its spend-based charging model: 'SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP.' QuantumScape wants to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees by moving PR creation into SAP ERP while retaining an intake layer. Zip was evaluated previously and Benjamin 'thought it was a great solution,' but switching costs and internal factors prevented adoption. Zip is not currently active in this evaluation cycle but will likely be invited to the RFP when the Ariba renewal forces evaluation. The competitive wedge is intake-only optionality: Zycus can provide intake/orchestration only (lower switching cost than full P2P migration) with iSaaS middleware or API integration to the relevant stakeholder or Ariba, eliminating Ariba spend-based fees without requiring full Ariba rip-out. Benjamin validated this fit: 'we would maybe be looking for something that is just handling the intake, but not the post PO and the invoice automation and all of that stuff.' How we win: (1) position intake-only option as lowest-risk, lowest-switching-cost path (vs. Ariba full rip-out or Zip full P2P); (2) emphasize SAP ERP integration via iSaaS to eliminate Ariba fees; (3) anchor Merlin's AI/Teams-native intake and intuitive UI as superior to Ariba and comparable to Zip; (4) multi-thread to CFO/VP Finance to quantify Ariba cost savings and build the business case for migration.

sources: Avoma notes: 'Decentralized model: engineers/scientists execute full purchasing...causing control gaps' and 'Primary driver to change: Ariba charges based on spend in platform; intent to move PR/PO flow into SAP to avoid those fees' (26 Jun)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is clearly identified and owned by Benjamin Sheres (Procurement Operations Manager). The primary operational pain is a decentralized purchasing model where engineers and scientists execute full purchasing (requisitions, supplier onboarding, follow-up), causing procurement to audit requisitions and creating control gaps. Benjamin stated on 26 Jun: 'we give the power to basically handle the full purchasing process to the engineers and the scientists...as a result of that, we as a procurement organization have been needing to audit them and make sure that they are making the requisition right...we're trying to move from giving them a ton of power to centralizing it. But the difficulty is that we don't have a ton of buyers to be able to help run the whole process for them.' The financial pain is SAP Ariba's spend-based charging model: 'SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP.' QuantumScape wants to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees by moving PR creation into SAP ERP while retaining an intake layer to funnel non-catalog requests to procurement. The compelling event is the upcoming Ariba renewal (date unknown), which will force a decision: renew Ariba (status quo, high cost) or migrate to a new intake platform (lower cost, better control). Benjamin is the pain owner and process champion, but he lacks budget authority—CFO or VP Finance owns the commercial decision.

sources: Avoma note: 'Recent IT turnover noted, affecting platform-change bandwidth and reopening evaluation' (26 Jun)
paper_process.narrative: Paper process (contracting, legal, procurement mechanics, InfoSec, ERP integration) is unknown. No discussion of MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel, or InfoSec requirements occurred on the 26 Jun discovery call. Benjamin's focus was on intake/orchestration functionality and SAP ERP integration optionality. When the Ariba renewal triggers formal evaluation and QuantumScape runs an RFP, the paper process will include: (1) RFP round (vendor shortlisting, demos, pricing); (2) commercial negotiation (SaaS pricing, implementation services, term length); (3) IT integration scoping (iSaaS middleware or API integration to the relevant stakeholder/Ariba); (4) InfoSec review (standard for enterprise SaaS); (5) legal/contracting (MSA, SOW, redlining); (6) CFO/finance approval (budget and business case sign-off). None of these stages have begun. The implementation timeline will be constrained by limited internal IT/procurement bandwidth and recent IT turnover (Benjamin stated 'we've had some turnover in our IT organization' and 'team lacks bandwidth for a project now' on 26 Jun). Positioning low-touch, self-service configuration (which Benjamin validated as a fit: 'looks relatively intuitive') will be critical to address bandwidth constraints.

decision_process.narrative: Decision process is unknown. Benjamin Sheres stated on 26 Jun: 'at some point, there may be a breaking point where...let's really run a serious RFP. We're gonna look at all the different intake providers, and we're gonna really invest and transition away. But I don't think that we're there right now.' This confirms that when the Ariba renewal forces evaluation, QuantumScape will run a formal RFP comparing intake providers. However, the approval chain, stakeholder roles, and decision timeline are not yet defined. Benjamin is a technical contact (Procurement Operations Manager) with no budget authority, so the process will escalate to CFO/VP Finance (likely Kevin Hettrich, Jin Qian, or Asha Yang) for budget approval, IT leadership (Michael Hoang, Anil Nair, Sudheer Malepati) for integration/implementation scoping, and potentially Supply Chain/Procurement leadership (Alison Levy, Director Supply Chain) for operational sign-off. No multi-threading has occurred, so Zycus has no visibility into the buying committee composition, decision-maker hierarchy, or approval gates. The Ariba renewal date is the forcing function, but that date is unknown to the relevant stakeholder stated 'our renewal is coming up pretty quickly' but gave no specific date.

sources: Avoma notes: 'Requirements highlighted: conversational AI intake (Teams), OCR/quote parsing, supplier recommendations, punch-outs, PR drafting, approval workflow, budget/PO change-order checks, iSaaS middleware and APIs for ERP integration' (26 Jun)
decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are partially surfaced from the discovery call but not yet formalized or validated by the buying committee. Benjamin stated on 26 Jun that the primary requirement is 'an efficient intake/orchestration platform only (not necessarily PO-to-invoice automation); capture requests and draft PRs for ERP.' Key evaluation criteria Benjamin highlighted during the Merlin demo include: (1) conversational AI intake via the relevant stakeholder (QuantumScape is a Teams shop); (2) OCR and AI quote parsing to extract line items and vendor data from uploaded quotes; (3) supplier recommendations (catalog/punch-out bias plus preferred-supplier matching); (4) PR drafting with approval workflow and budget/PO change-order checks; (5) iSaaS middleware and API integration to push PRs into SAP ERP or Ariba without requiring full P2P migration; (6) intuitive, relatively easy form/configuration and self-service after initial implementation (Benjamin emphasized 'we don't have a ton of buyers...we need tools to set them up for success'). Benjamin validated these features positively ('that was all interesting', 'looks relatively intuitive') and acknowledged Merlin compares favorably to Zip. However, no formal RFP, BRD, or multi-stakeholder validation has occurred. The criteria are inferred from Benjamin's use-case discussion, not confirmed by a buying committee or Decision Maker. When the Ariba renewal forces evaluation, QuantumScape will likely run a formal RFP and codify criteria across IT, procurement, and finance stakeholders.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba (incumbent) is the primary barrier, not a live competitive threat—this is an incumbent displacement scenario driven by Ariba's spend-based charging model pain. Benjamin stated significant prior Ariba investment makes switching difficult ('we've already made significant investment in getting Ariba working'), but the pain is real and the Ariba renewal will force re-evaluation. Zip was evaluated previously and Benjamin 'thought it was a great solution,' but switching costs and internal factors blocked adoption. Zip is dormant now but will likely be invited to the RFP when the renewal triggers. The strongest current threat is not a competitor—it is INERTIA and switching cost. Ariba renewal date unknown; without visibility into that date, Zycus cannot time re-engagement. Competitive wedge: intake-only optionality (lower switching cost than full P2P migration) plus SAP ERP integration via iSaaS to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees without full Ariba rip-out.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: We currently use SAP Ariba and it is what it is... current motivation to change: move away from SAP Ariba due to spend-based charges and reliance on Ariba investments... need suppliers to be able to confirm orders, update lead time, send e-invoices without having to set up an account or even worse pay for an enterprise account (dealbreaker)
competitors.source: Avoma call 26 Jun, Benjamin Sheres; email 25 Jun
competitors.how_we_win: Intake-only optionality (lower switching cost than full Ariba replacement) + SAP ERP integration that moves PR creation into SAP to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees + portal-free supplier enablement (the dealbreaker Ariba cannot solve). Position Merlin Intake as the low-risk layer that works WITH SAP, not a rip-and-replace.

competitors.name: Zip
competitors.quote: Honestly, in my opinion, I thought it was a great solution. But internal organizational factors made it difficult to switch platforms because we've already made significant investment in getting Ariba working.
competitors.how_we_win: Zip was viewed positively but lost to Ariba inertia in prior evaluation. Likely to resurface in future RFP. Anchor Merlin's comparable AI/Teams-native intake and intuitive UI as on par with Zip, plus emphasize Zycus full-suite optionality and SAP integration depth as differentiators vs. Zip (intake-only pure-play).

competitors.name: SAP Ariba (Guide Buying)
competitors.quote: SAP Ariba charges you based on how much spend you're ordering in that platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP. We've already made significant investment in getting Ariba working, and internal organizational factors made it difficult to switch platforms.
competitors.how_we_win: Position intake-only option with SAP ERP integration via iSaaS as lowest-risk path to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees without full Ariba rip-out; quantify Ariba cost savings and ROI to CFO/VP Finance; emphasize Merlin's AI/Teams-native intake as superior to Ariba's clunky UI.

competitors.name: Do Nothing (Ariba renewal default)
competitors.quote: Our renewal is coming up pretty quickly, and I don't think that we're gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now. At some point, there may be a breaking point where let's really run a serious RFP, but I don't think that we're there right now.
competitors.how_we_win: The highest current threat is not a competitor—it is inertia. Without EB/DM alignment and a quantified business case, the Ariba renewal may default to status quo despite Benjamin's interest. Multi-thread to CFO/VP Finance to quantify Ariba cost savings, ROI, and procurement efficiency gains; position intake-only migration as low-risk, low-switching-cost vs. full Ariba rip-out.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong. QuantumScape's primary requirement is conversational AI intake via Microsoft Teams, and Benjamin validated Merlin's generative AI and agentic intake capabilities positively on 26 Jun ('I like that it lives within Teams', 'looks relatively intuitive'). Key AI features Benjamin highlighted: conversational AI parses user requests to determine goods vs. services and extract metadata for PRs, OCR + AI quote parsing extracts line items and vendor data, supplier recommendations via AI search, approval workflow and budget/PO change-order checks. Benjamin acknowledged Merlin's AI/Teams-native intake compares favorably to Zip. No AIS score in Salesforce, but buyer explicitly seeks AI-driven intake modernization. Not under-positioned—AI is the core value prop and Benjamin validated it.

items.quote: I like that it lives within Teams, which we are a Teams organization.
items.criterion: Conversational AI intake via Microsoft Teams (QuantumScape is a Teams shop)

items.quote: How trustworthy is it to take the right information and plug it in? I would want somebody to verify, but the OCR is really good.

items.quote: How did you configure the backend to guide them towards the purchasing that the procurement organization wants them to use, like preferred IT vendor? The system promotes catalogs/punch-outs when available and offers internal preferred-supplier matches.
items.criterion: Supplier recommendations (catalog/punch-out bias plus preferred-supplier matching)

items.quote: Tell me a little bit more about how the approval workflows are configured. Is there smart change order approval logic that will kind of handle exceptions? You have budget visibility against POs and exception logic that enforces additional approvals when budget or thresholds are exceeded.
items.criterion: PR drafting with approval workflow and budget/PO change-order checks

items.quote: We would maybe be looking for something that is just handling the intake, but not the post PO and the invoice automation. We can provide just the intake, and we have integration tools via iSaaS middleware and APIs.
items.criterion: iSaaS middleware and API integration to push PRs into SAP ERP or Ariba without requiring full P2P migration

items.quote: I was just getting a sense of things because some other intake platforms is just really difficult to set up or they're not intuitive. But the way that this looks is looks relatively intuitive.
items.criterion: Intuitive, relatively easy form/configuration and self-service after initial implementation

customer_expectations_fit.summary: QuantumScape's stated requirements align well with Merlin Intake's core capabilities. Benjamin validated fit positively on 26 Jun discovery call, acknowledging Teams-native intake, intuitive UI, OCR/quote parsing, supplier recommendations, PR drafting, approval workflow, and SAP ERP integration via iSaaS. Key alignment: QuantumScape wants intake-only (not full P2P), which Zycus can provide. Key gap: no formal BRD, RFP, or multi-stakeholder validation yet—criteria are inferred from Benjamin's use-case discussion, not confirmed by buying committee.

value.how_we_win: Position intake-only option with SAP ERP integration via iSaaS as lowest-risk path to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees without full Ariba rip-out; quantify Ariba cost savings and ROI to CFO/VP Finance; emphasize Merlin's AI/Teams-native intake as superior to Ariba's clunky UI.

packets.subject: SAP Ariba (Guide Buying)

value.name: Do Nothing (Ariba renewal default)
value.quote: Our renewal is coming up pretty quickly, and I don't think that we're gonna have the bandwidth to do a project right now. At some point, there may be a breaking point where let's really run a serious RFP, but I don't think that we're there right now.
value.how_we_win: The highest current threat is not a competitor—it is inertia. Without EB/DM alignment and a quantified business case, the Ariba renewal may default to status quo despite Benjamin's interest. Multi-thread to CFO/VP Finance to quantify Ariba cost savings, ROI, and procurement efficiency gains; position intake-only migration as low-risk, low-switching-cost vs. full Ariba rip-out.

business_case.evidence: Pain articulated but not quantified. Benjamin stated primary driver is 'SAP Ariba charges based on spend in platform, we wanna move away from that and go into SAP' to eliminate Ariba spend-based fees. Operational pain: decentralized purchasing model causes procurement audit overhead and control gaps ('we as a procurement organization have been needing to audit them...we don't have a ton of buyers'). No ROI, savings, or automation impact quantified.

gaps: SAP Ariba renewal date unknown—Benjamin stated 'our renewal is coming up pretty quickly' but gave no date. Entire deal timing depends on renewal forcing evaluation; without visibility into renewal date, Zycus cannot time re-engagement or proposal effectively.
Economic Buyer not identified—CFO Kevin Hettrich, VP Finance Jin Qian, or Group CFO Asha Yang likely control Ariba renewal decision and budget approval, but none engaged in this opp yet.

Decision Maker and buying committee composition unknown—no visibility into stakeholder roles, approval hierarchy, or decision timeline; Benjamin stated future RFP will involve 'looking at all the different intake providers' but gave no detail.
No quantified business case or ROI model built—Benjamin stated Ariba spend-based fees are the primary pain but did not quantify savings; no CFO-ready business case exists to justify migration.
